title: Chapter 3: Contraband
content: The supply closet on the ground floor was locked, but locks, Chloe had learned at age sixteen when her parents first tried to confiscate her laptop, were a suggestion.

She'd spent her second day mapping the institute. Not metaphorically — actually mapping. In her head, since they'd taken her phone, but the spatial memory that had once helped her navigate a twelve-hour music festival on two hours of sleep turned out to be just as useful in a psychiatric institution.

Ground floor: lobby, cafeteria, therapy rooms (Group A through D), staff offices, supply closet. First floor: patient rooms, medication station, nurses' station. Second floor: control room, administration, Dr. Sterling's office.

The supply closet was at the end of a hallway that connected the cafeteria to the therapy wing. It was locked with a standard keypad — the kind you could buy at Home Depot. Not biometric. Not even RFID.
*Amateurs*, Chloe thought.

The keypad had four digits. Most staff used their birth year. She'd watched three orderlies enter it during her "orientation walks" — the ones where they paraded you past the facilities like a real estate agent showing a condo, conveniently skipping anything that looked like a locked ward.
One-nine-seven-eight. One-nine-eight-two. One-nine-six-five.
A pattern. All boomers. All the same logic: nobody would guess a year that old.

Chloe waited until the hallway was empty — not hard, since the institute ran on a schedule so rigid you could set your watch to it. Everyone was in Group Therapy B. Everyone except her, because she'd told Nurse Holloway she was having "a processing moment" and needed to journal.
Holloway had actually smiled. *Progress*, her expression said.
Chloe had written exactly one sentence in her journal: *THEY DON'T CHECK THE JOURNALS.*
She punched in one-nine-six-six.
The lock clicked.

The supply closet was bigger than her Brooklyn apartment. Shelves of cleaning supplies, spare linens, broken equipment tagged for repair. And in the back corner, exactly where Layla had said it would be: a stack of old tablets.

Not iPads. Older. Institutional surplus — the kind a school district buys in bulk and forgets to update. But they had batteries. They had screens. They had, crucially, Wi-Fi antennae that might still work.
Chloe grabbed two tablets. The screens were scratched but functional. She shoved them under her hoodie and slipped back into the hallway.
Two minutes later she was in her room. Ninety seconds after that, Layla was at her door.
"You got them." Not a question.
Chloe handed her one of the tablets. Layla turned it over, her expression shifting from clinical to something warmer.
"Android 4.4," Layla said. "Ancient. But the Wi-Fi chip is good." Her fingers were already moving. "Give me a day. I can get root on these."
"Can you get us internet?"
"Can you keep the cameras off me while I work?"
Chloe grinned. "Bestie. That's what I do."

The next three days were a masterclass in institutional jiu-jitsu. Chloe discovered that "processing moments" could be extended indefinitely if you looked sad enough. Orderlies changed shifts at 6 PM and 6 AM — during shift change, camera monitoring dropped to one person watching fourteen screens. The cafeteria had a back entrance used by kitchen staff who didn't live on-site and didn't care enough to check IDs.
Day four: Layla had root. She'd set up a proxy through the institute's own network. The tablets couldn't reach the open internet, but they could access the institute's internal messaging system.
And the internal messaging system had email.
"I can send," Layla said. "Can't receive. But I can push messages out."
"Who can you reach?"
"Anyone."
Chloe sat on her bed. The camera blinked its red heartbeat.
"Okay," she said. "Here's what we do."
But the network wasn't the first priority. The first priority was morale.

"These girls are dying," Chloe told Layla after their third secret meeting in the laundry room — the only place in the institute where the industrial dryers ran loud enough to drown out conversation. "Not physically. Spiritually. Nobody's told them the truth about why they're here."
"What truth?"
"That they're not sick. They're inconvenient."
Layla was quiet for a moment. "You want to start a group."
"I want to start a revolution. But a group is step one."
It started with Sophia.

Chloe found her in the common room at 2 AM, staring at the black screen of the communal television. The institute ran on a schedule, but insomniacs weren't punished — Holloway considered sleeplessness a symptom of progress. Breaking the circadian rhythm was part of the process.
"Can't sleep?" Chloe asked.
Sophia didn't look up. "I haven't slept more than three hours a night since I got here."
"The meds?"
"The meds make me sleep. That's why I don't take them."
Chloe sat down beside her. For a long moment, neither of them spoke.
"You know why you're really here, right?" Chloe said.
Sophia's jaw tightened. "My mother says I have an eating disorder."
"Do you?"
"I have a not-wanting-to-marry-a-forty-seven-year-old disorder." Sophia's voice was perfectly flat. "But that's not in the DSM."
"Funny how that works."
Sophia finally looked at her. "You're the new one. The one who called her dad a venture capitalist on TikTok."
"Guilty."
"Five million views."
"Five point two."
Sophia's mouth twitched — the ghost of a smile. "Why aren't you broken yet?"

"Because I realized something," Chloe said. "This place runs on the assumption that we believe we're sick. That we think we deserve to be here. But we're not sick. We're the normal ones. They're the ones who think it's reasonable to institutionalize your daughter for being embarrassing on the internet."
Sophia was quiet for a long time.
"What do you want from me?" she asked.
"Nothing," Chloe said. "I just want you to know you're not alone. There's three of us now. Me, Layla — the hacker girl — and you, if you want in."
"In on what?"
Chloe smiled. "On making this place very, very sorry it ever admitted us."
